body:
# Stripe Checkout Implementation Rules

Cursor rules for integrating Stripe Checkout in a Next.js App Router project. Every rule addresses a real gotcha that costs teams hours on first integration.

## Install

```bash
mkdir -p .cursor/rules
# Save the files below into .cursor/rules/
```

## Rule 1: checkout-session.mdc

```mdc
---
description: Creating a Stripe Checkout session
globs: '**/checkout/**,**/api/checkout/**'
alwaysApply: false
---

# Stripe Checkout session creation

- Checkout sessions are created server-side only. Never expose your secret key to the client.
- `success_url` and `cancel_url` must be absolute URLs. Use `process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL`.
- Always include `client_reference_id` tied to your internal user ID. This is how you match the session back to your DB in the webhook.
- For subscriptions, also set `metadata.plan` so the webhook doesn't need to re-query the price.
- For one-time purchases, the `line_items` array defines what's being sold. Use `price` (a Stripe Price ID), not `amount`, for recurring and most one-time cases.
- Set `automatic_tax.enabled: true` if you're using Stripe Tax (requires Stripe Tax subscription).
- Return the Checkout URL, let the client redirect via `window.location.href`. Do NOT use Next.js redirect() in a Server Action for this — Stripe's URL is external and the Next redirect wrapper may not work across domains the same way.
```

## Rule 2: webhook-handler.mdc

```mdc
---
description: Stripe webhook handler in Next.js App Router
globs: '**/api/webhooks/stripe/**'
alwaysApply: true
---

# Stripe webhook handler

- Always verify signature: `stripe.webhooks.constructEvent(body, sig, secret)`
- The body MUST be the raw request body. In Next.js App Router route handlers, use `await req.text()` — NOT `await req.json()`.
- Wrap the handler's core logic in a try/catch. Return 200 on success, 400 on signature failure, 500 on your own code failure.
- Idempotency: Stripe retries failed webhooks. Store `event.id` in a database table and check before processing. If you've seen it, return 200 without re-processing.
- Handle at least: `checkout.session.completed`, `invoice.payment_succeeded`, `invoice.payment_failed`, `customer.subscription.deleted`, `customer.subscription.updated`.
- Update your DB in a single transaction. If the transaction fails, return 500 and Stripe will retry.
- Never await non-critical side effects (email, analytics) in the webhook handler path — queue them for background processing.
```

## Rule 3: test-vs-live.mdc

```mdc
---
description: Stripe test vs live environment separation
globs: '**'
alwaysApply: false
---

# Test vs Live keys

- Use `sk_test_*` and `pk_test_*` in development and preview environments.
- Use `sk_live_*` and `pk_live_*` in production only.
- Webhook signing secrets differ between test and live — set `STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET_TEST` and `STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET_LIVE` separately.
- Always match the API version in your Stripe library to the one configured in your Stripe dashboard. Mismatched versions cause subtle shape differences in event payloads.
- Never hardcode keys. Always `process.env.STRIPE_SECRET_KEY`.
```

## Rule 4: retry-and-timeouts.mdc

```mdc
---
description: Retry logic and timeouts for Stripe API calls
globs: '**'
alwaysApply: false
---

# Retries and timeouts

- Stripe's Node library retries idempotent requests automatically. You don't need to wrap in a retry loop.
- For non-idempotent operations (like creating a Checkout session), use `stripe.idempotencyKey` to prevent duplicates if you retry manually.
- Set a reasonable timeout: `new Stripe(key, { timeout: 10000 })` — default is 80s which is too long for a web request.
- For webhook handlers, your OWN response time matters. Stripe times out at 30s and retries. Keep handlers under 3s.
```

## Rule 5: portal-and-subscription-management.mdc

```mdc
---
description: Customer portal for self-serve subscription management
globs: '**/api/portal/**'
alwaysApply: false
---

# Customer portal

- Use `stripe.billingPortal.sessions.create({ customer, return_url })` to generate a portal session for the user.
- Configure the portal features in the Stripe Dashboard first — which actions (cancel, update payment method, update plan) are allowed.
- Return the portal URL and redirect the user.
- Portal events fire webhooks too — handle `customer.subscription.updated` and `customer.subscription.deleted`.
- Never try to replicate the portal UI yourself. The portal handles dunning, failed payments, 3DS, and tax — hand-rolling this is months of work.
```

## Rule 6: the six gotchas.mdc

```mdc
---
description: Six common Stripe Checkout bugs
globs: '**'
alwaysApply: false
---

# The six gotchas

## 1. Parsing the webhook body instead of reading it raw

WRONG: `const event = JSON.parse(await req.json())`
RIGHT: `const body = await req.text(); const event = stripe.webhooks.constructEvent(body, sig, secret)`

Parsing corrupts the body for signature verification.

## 2. Not using client_reference_id

Without it, matching a completed session back to your internal user requires either the email (unreliable) or searching by `customer_details`. Always pass `client_reference_id`.

## 3. Trusting webhook order

Webhooks can arrive out of order. If `checkout.session.completed` arrives after `invoice.payment_succeeded`, handle both idempotently.

## 4. Missing the trailing slash on URLs

Some Stripe webhooks pass URLs with a trailing slash, others without. Normalize before comparison.

## 5. Not saving the customer ID

First checkout creates a Stripe Customer. Save the ID immediately — you'll need it for the portal, for refunds, for everything.

## 6. Testing only the happy path

Simulate: a failed payment, a cancelled subscription, a disputed charge, a subscription update, a customer email change. Use the Stripe Dashboard's "Send test webhook" feature during development.
```
